---
title: "Chi-Square, Student's t, and F"
subtitle: "Core Sample · Probability Foundations"
---

```{python}
#| label: setup
#| include: false
import numpy as np
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
from scipy import stats

# A consistent, restrained visual style for every figure in this article.
plt.rcParams.update({
    "figure.figsize": (6.2, 4.0),
    "figure.dpi": 140,
    "axes.spines.top": False,
    "axes.spines.right": False,
    "axes.grid": True,
    "grid.alpha": 0.25,
    "font.size": 11,
    "axes.titlesize": 12,
    "lines.linewidth": 2.0,
})

CHI   = "#2b3a67"     # chi-square family
TCOL  = "#c1121f"     # t family
FCOL  = "#0a7d4d"     # F family
NORM  = "#6c2d8f"     # the normal, for contrast
GREY  = "#6c757d"
ACCENT = "#d97706"
```

> **Where this sits in the track.** This is the last manufactured-distribution article in
> the catalog, and the one the inference machinery downstream actually runs on. It assumes
> the **normal** distribution and its standard form $Z\sim\mathcal N(0,1)$ from
> [PROB-13 *The Normal (Gaussian) Distribution*]; the **gamma** function and the
> **gamma density** from [PROB-14 *Gamma, Exponential, and Beta*] (we restate exactly
> what we borrow and derive everything chi-square-specific from it); **independence and
> the i.i.d. sample** from [PROB-08], which lets us split a numerator from a denominator;
> and **expectation/variance** from [PROB-05]–[PROB-06]. It sets up the entire testing
> apparatus of the downstream tracks: **Statistical Inference** C2 (the $t$ and
> $\chi^2$ pivots), D2 ($z/t/\chi^2/F$ tests), and **Regression** R9 (the $F$-test for
> nested models). After this the catalog closes with [PROB-16 *The Exponential Family*].

## Abstract {.unnumbered}

Three distributions run almost every classical hypothesis test, and none of them is a
model for data in the wild — they are the distributions of *statistics computed from
normal data*. The **chi-square** is what you get when you square independent standard
normals and add them; it is the distribution of a sample's spread. **Student's $t$** is a
standard normal divided by the square root of an independent, rescaled chi-square; it is
what the $z$-score becomes when you must *estimate* the denominator instead of knowing it,
and that single substitution is what fattens its tails. The **$F$** is a ratio of two
rescaled chi-squares; it is how we compare two estimates of spread, and how we ask whether
a richer model explains more than chance. This article builds all three from the normal —
every density, every mean and variance, every degrees-of-freedom bookkeeping step on the
page — and shows the one structural fact that ties them together: $t^2$ *is* an $F$, and
both are chi-squares in disguise. The single claim it defends is that **these are not three
unrelated formula sheets but one family, generated by squaring, summing, and ratioing
normals**, and that knowing which is which tells you exactly which uncertainty you are
accounting for.

# The Core {#sec-core}

::: {.callout-important title="The Core"}
Start with independent standard normals $Z_1,\dots,Z_k$. Square them and add:
$\chi^2_k = Z_1^2+\dots+Z_k^2$ — the **chi-square** with $k$ degrees of freedom, the
distribution of *squared distance from the mean*, i.e. of **variance**. Now build two more
out of it. Divide a standard normal by the square root of an independent chi-square that
has been divided by its degrees of freedom:
$$
t_k = \frac{Z}{\sqrt{\chi^2_k/k}} \qquad\text{— Student's } t.
$$
This is the $z$-score with its denominator *estimated rather than known*, and that
estimation is what makes its tails heavy. Finally, take the ratio of two independent
chi-squares, each divided by its own degrees of freedom:
$$
F_{d_1,d_2} = \frac{\chi^2_{d_1}/d_1}{\chi^2_{d_2}/d_2} \qquad\text{— the } F.
$$
This compares two variances. The three are one family: $t_k^2 = F_{1,k}$, and every $F$ is
a ratio of chi-squares, which are sums of squared normals. **Everything orbits the normal.**
:::

Everything below unpacks that box. The names are intimidating and the densities look
baroque, but the *construction* is simple arithmetic on normals — square, sum, divide,
ratio — and each operation answers a concrete question about a sample. Hold onto one
sentence: **these distributions describe statistics, not data.** You never collect
chi-square-distributed observations the way you collect heights or waiting times. You
compute a sample variance, a $t$-statistic, an $F$-ratio, and *those* numbers follow these
laws. That reframing is the whole point of the catalog ending here: PROB-11 through PROB-14
gave us models for the world; PROB-15 gives us the distributions of the tools we use to
interrogate those models.

# Why we are here: the problem that forced these distributions {#sec-why}

Keep one question in view for the whole article: **once data is normal, what is the
distribution of the things we actually compute from it?** Every derivation below answers a
piece of that. If a step feels arbitrary, return here.

## The original problem: small samples and an unknown spread

By the late nineteenth century, statisticians had the normal distribution and they had a
beautiful result for the sample mean: if observations are normal with mean $\mu$ and
variance $\sigma^2$, the sample mean $\bar X = \frac1n\sum X_i$ is normal with mean $\mu$
and variance $\sigma^2/n$. So
$$
Z = \frac{\bar X - \mu}{\sigma/\sqrt n} \sim \mathcal N(0,1),
$$
a clean standard normal, and you could turn it into a statement about $\mu$ by reading off
normal tail probabilities. There was a fatal practical catch: **the formula contains
$\sigma$, the true population standard deviation, which you never know.** In real work you
have the data and nothing else. You must estimate $\sigma$ from the same sample, replacing
it with the sample standard deviation $s$. The natural move is to write
$$
T = \frac{\bar X - \mu}{s/\sqrt n}
$$
and pretend $T$ is still standard normal. For large samples that is nearly true. For the
small samples that dominated real laboratory and field work — agriculture, brewing,
biometry, where $n$ might be 4 or 8 — it is badly wrong, and *trusting the normal tables
makes you overconfident*: you declare findings significant that are not.

The man who fixed this was **William Sealy Gosset**, a chemist at the Guinness brewery in
Dublin, working on small batches of barley and yeast where large samples were a luxury he
did not have. Guinness forbade employees from publishing (to protect trade secrets), so in
1908 Gosset published "The Probable Error of a Mean" under the pseudonym **"Student."** He
worked out, by a mix of derivation and ingenious simulation (rolling thousands of cards
with measured criminals' heights), the exact distribution of $T$ when $\sigma$ is
estimated. It is *not* the normal: it has heavier tails, and the smaller the sample, the
heavier. That distribution is **Student's $t$**, and it is the reason every introductory
statistics course teaches a "$t$-test" rather than a "$z$-test" for the small samples that
real experiments produce.

But $t$ has a hidden dependency. Its denominator is built from the sample variance, and to
know the distribution of $t$ you first need the distribution of the sample variance itself.
That distribution — the sum of squared deviations of normal data — is the **chi-square**,
named and studied by **Karl Pearson** in 1900 for his goodness-of-fit test, and traceable
to **Friedrich Robert Helmert** in the 1870s, who first derived the distribution of the
sample variance of normal observations. And once you can describe one sample's variance,
the obvious next question is how to *compare two* variances, or to ask whether the extra
structure in a complicated model reduces variance more than chance would. That comparison
is a ratio of chi-squares — the **$F$ distribution**, named by George Snedecor in the 1930s
in honor of **Ronald Fisher**, who built it into the analysis of variance.

So the three distributions are a chain of necessities. Estimating a mean's uncertainty
forces you to estimate a variance ($\Rightarrow$ chi-square). Plugging that estimated
variance into the $z$-score forces a new distribution ($\Rightarrow$ $t$). Comparing two
such variances, or two models, forces yet another ($\Rightarrow$ $F$). Each is the price of
not knowing $\sigma$. That is the *why* we will hold onto: **these are the distributions of
estimating spread, and of everything you must do once you accept that spread is estimated,
not given.**

::: {.callout-tip title="You might be asked"}
*"Why isn't $\bar X - \mu$ over $s/\sqrt n$ just normal? It's a sum of normals over a
number."* Because $s$ is **not a number** — it is itself a random quantity computed from
the same data, and it varies from sample to sample. When the denominator is a known
constant $\sigma$, you have normal-over-constant, which is normal. When the denominator is
random, you have normal-over-random-square-root, and the extra randomness in the
denominator both widens the spread and, crucially, *fattens the tails*: an occasional small
$s$ in the denominator produces an occasional huge $T$. Quantifying exactly that is the
content of @sec-t. This is the single most common conceptual slip about the $t$, and naming
it precisely — "the denominator is random" — is what an interviewer is listening for.
:::

# Intuition before symbols {#sec-intuition}

Before any algebra, three pictures.

**Chi-square is squared distance.** Drop $k$ independent standard-normal darts on a number
line — or, better, think of one dart in $k$-dimensional space whose coordinates are
$Z_1,\dots,Z_k$. The squared distance of that dart from the origin is
$Z_1^2+\dots+Z_k^2$. That is a chi-square with $k$ degrees of freedom. Because squaring
kills the sign, a chi-square lives on $[0,\infty)$; because it is a *sum* of $k$
nonnegative pieces, it drifts to the right as $k$ grows (more pieces, bigger total), and by
the same logic — many independent contributions adding up — it grows more symmetric and
bell-like as $k$ increases. Its center sits at $k$ (each squared standard normal averages
$1$, and there are $k$ of them).

**$t$ is a normal whose ruler wobbles.** Picture the standard normal $Z$. Now imagine you
cannot measure it against a fixed ruler of length $\sigma$; you must use a ruler whose
length you estimated from a few measurements, so the ruler itself is a bit too long or too
short on any given day. Dividing $Z$ by that wobbly ruler ($\sqrt{\chi^2_k/k}$, which
averages $1$ but scatters around it) gives you $t$. On days the ruler reads short, $Z$ gets
magnified — producing the occasional extreme value that the fixed-ruler normal never would.
That is why the $t$ curve looks like a normal that has been *pinched lower in the middle and
fattened in the tails*. As $k\to\infty$ the ruler stops wobbling (you have effectively
infinite data to estimate it), and $t$ becomes the normal.

**$F$ is a fairness scale for two spreads.** Put two independent estimates of variance on
the two pans of a balance, each first divided by its degrees of freedom so they are on equal
footing. If the two truly measure the same underlying spread, the ratio hovers around $1$.
If the numerator's spread is genuinely larger — because a model captured real structure, or
because two groups really differ in variability — the ratio climbs above $1$, and the
$F$-distribution tells you how far above $1$ is surprising.

```{python}
#| label: fig-three-families
#| fig-cap: "The three families, each generated from normals. **Left:** chi-square densities for $k=1,2,4,8$ degrees of freedom — supported on $[0,\\infty)$, right-skewed, drifting right and growing symmetric as $k$ rises (its mean is $k$). **Middle:** the $t$ density for $k=1,3,30$ against the standard normal (purple dashed) — same bell shape, but lower-peaked and heavier-tailed for small $k$, collapsing onto the normal as $k\\to\\infty$. **Right:** $F$ densities for a few $(d_1,d_2)$ — right-skewed, concentrated near $1$ when both degrees of freedom are large, which is the 'no real difference' baseline."
fig, (axA, axB, axC) = plt.subplots(1, 3, figsize=(11.5, 3.6))

# chi-square
xc = np.linspace(0.001, 18, 400)
for k, a in zip([1, 2, 4, 8], [0.45, 0.65, 0.85, 1.0]):
    axA.plot(xc, stats.chi2.pdf(xc, k), color=CHI, alpha=a, label=f"$k={k}$")
axA.set_ylim(0, 0.55); axA.set_xlabel("$x$"); axA.set_ylabel("density")
axA.set_title(r"Chi-square $\chi^2_k$"); axA.legend(fontsize=8)

# t
xt = np.linspace(-5, 5, 400)
for k, a in zip([1, 3, 30], [0.45, 0.72, 1.0]):
    axB.plot(xt, stats.t.pdf(xt, k), color=TCOL, alpha=a, label=f"$k={k}$")
axB.plot(xt, stats.norm.pdf(xt), color=NORM, ls="--", lw=1.6, label=r"$\mathcal N(0,1)$")
axB.set_xlabel("$t$"); axB.set_ylabel("density")
axB.set_title("Student's $t_k$"); axB.legend(fontsize=8)

# F
xf = np.linspace(0.001, 4, 400)
for (d1, d2), a in zip([(2, 8), (5, 12), (10, 30)], [0.5, 0.75, 1.0]):
    axC.plot(xf, stats.f.pdf(xf, d1, d2), color=FCOL, alpha=a, label=f"$d_1={d1},d_2={d2}$")
axC.axvline(1, color=GREY, ls=":", lw=1.2)
axC.set_xlabel("$x$"); axC.set_ylabel("density")
axC.set_title(r"$F_{d_1,d_2}$"); axC.legend(fontsize=8)
plt.tight_layout()
```

# What we borrow from the gamma, stated precisely {#sec-borrow}

The chi-square is a special case of the gamma distribution, and the cleanest derivations
ride on that fact. The gamma is built in full in [PROB-14]; here we **import exactly two
ingredients and nothing more**, state them precisely, and derive everything else.

::: {.callout-note title="Imported from [PROB-14], used as stated"}
**(i) The gamma function.** For $\alpha>0$,
$$
\Gamma(\alpha) = \int_0^\infty u^{\alpha-1}e^{-u}\,du,
$$ {#eq-gammafn}
with the two facts we will use: the **recurrence** $\Gamma(\alpha)=(\alpha-1)\,\Gamma(\alpha-1)$
(so $\Gamma(n)=(n-1)!$ for integer $n$), and the **half-integer seed**
$\Gamma(\tfrac12)=\sqrt{\pi}$. (That last equals the Gaussian integral
$\int_{-\infty}^\infty e^{-z^2/2}dz=\sqrt{2\pi}$ rewritten; it is derived in [PROB-13].)

**(ii) The gamma density.** A random variable $W$ is **Gamma$(\alpha,\beta)$** with *shape*
$\alpha>0$ and *rate* $\beta>0$ if its density is
$$
f_W(w) = \frac{\beta^{\alpha}}{\Gamma(\alpha)}\, w^{\alpha-1} e^{-\beta w},
\qquad w>0,
$$ {#eq-gammadens}
and $f_W(w)=0$ for $w\le 0$. Its mean is $\mathbb E[W]=\alpha/\beta$ and its variance is
$\operatorname{Var}(W)=\alpha/\beta^2$, and **the sum of independent gammas with the same
rate adds their shapes:** if $W_1\sim\text{Gamma}(\alpha_1,\beta)$ and
$W_2\sim\text{Gamma}(\alpha_2,\beta)$ are independent, then
$W_1+W_2\sim\text{Gamma}(\alpha_1+\alpha_2,\beta)$.
:::

Everything in this article is derived from these two boxes plus the normal density from
[PROB-13]. We do not assume the chi-square density — we *produce* it.

# The chi-square distribution {#sec-chisq}

## Definition by construction {#sec-chisq-def}

::: {#def-chisq}
**Definition (chi-square).** Let $Z_1,\dots,Z_k$ be independent standard-normal random
variables, $Z_i\sim\mathcal N(0,1)$. The **chi-square distribution with $k$ degrees of
freedom**, written $\chi^2_k$, is the distribution of
$$
Q = Z_1^2 + Z_2^2 + \dots + Z_k^2.
$$ {#eq-chisq-def}
The integer $k\ge 1$ is the **degrees of freedom** — literally the number of independent
squared normals being summed.
:::

We build the density in two steps: first the density of a *single* squared standard normal
($k=1$), then the sum.

## The density of one squared normal {#sec-chisq-one}

::: {#lem-chisq1}
**Lemma ($\chi^2_1$ is Gamma$(\tfrac12,\tfrac12)$).** If $Z\sim\mathcal N(0,1)$ then
$Q=Z^2$ has density
$$
f_Q(q) = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2\pi}}\, q^{-1/2} e^{-q/2}, \qquad q>0,
$$ {#eq-chisq1}
which is exactly the gamma density @eq-gammadens with shape $\alpha=\tfrac12$ and rate
$\beta=\tfrac12$.
:::

::: {.proof}
We go through the CDF, the safe fully-explicit route for a transform of a random variable
(the same technique [PROB-13] used to standardize). The standard-normal density is
$\varphi(z)=\frac{1}{\sqrt{2\pi}}e^{-z^2/2}$. For $q>0$, the event $\{Z^2\le q\}$ is exactly
the event $\{-\sqrt q \le Z \le \sqrt q\}$, because squaring is an increasing map on
nonnegative numbers and a decreasing map's mirror on the negatives. So the CDF of $Q$ is
$$
F_Q(q) = P(Z^2\le q) = P(-\sqrt q \le Z \le \sqrt q)
= \int_{-\sqrt q}^{\sqrt q} \varphi(z)\,dz.
$$
Because $\varphi$ is symmetric about $0$ ($\varphi(-z)=\varphi(z)$), the integral from
$-\sqrt q$ to $\sqrt q$ is twice the integral from $0$ to $\sqrt q$:
$$
F_Q(q) = 2\int_0^{\sqrt q}\varphi(z)\,dz.
$$
Now get the density by differentiating the CDF with respect to $q$ — this is where the
Calc-1 **fundamental theorem of calculus combined with the chain rule** does all the work.
Write $G(q)=\int_0^{\sqrt q}\varphi(z)\,dz$. The upper limit is $\sqrt q$, so by the chain
rule,
$$
\frac{d}{dq}\int_0^{\sqrt q}\varphi(z)\,dz
= \varphi(\sqrt q)\cdot \frac{d}{dq}\sqrt q
= \varphi(\sqrt q)\cdot \frac{1}{2\sqrt q}.
$$
The first factor is the integrand evaluated at the upper limit (fundamental theorem); the
second is the derivative of that upper limit, $\frac{d}{dq}q^{1/2}=\tfrac12 q^{-1/2}$.
Therefore
$$
f_Q(q) = F_Q'(q) = 2\cdot \varphi(\sqrt q)\cdot \frac{1}{2\sqrt q}
= \frac{\varphi(\sqrt q)}{\sqrt q}.
$$
Substitute $\varphi(\sqrt q)=\frac{1}{\sqrt{2\pi}}e^{-(\sqrt q)^2/2}=\frac{1}{\sqrt{2\pi}}e^{-q/2}$:
$$
f_Q(q) = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2\pi}}\, \frac{e^{-q/2}}{\sqrt q}
= \frac{1}{\sqrt{2\pi}}\, q^{-1/2} e^{-q/2},
$$
which is @eq-chisq1. Now match it to the gamma density @eq-gammadens with $\alpha=\tfrac12,
\beta=\tfrac12$. That density is
$$
\frac{\beta^{\alpha}}{\Gamma(\alpha)} w^{\alpha-1}e^{-\beta w}
= \frac{(1/2)^{1/2}}{\Gamma(1/2)}\, q^{-1/2} e^{-q/2}.
$$
The constant is $\frac{(1/2)^{1/2}}{\Gamma(1/2)} = \frac{1/\sqrt2}{\sqrt\pi}
= \frac{1}{\sqrt{2\pi}}$, using $\Gamma(\tfrac12)=\sqrt\pi$ from @eq-gammafn. The two
densities are identical term by term. $\qquad\blacksquare$
:::

This is the keystone. A single squared standard normal is a Gamma$(\tfrac12,\tfrac12)$, and
now the addition rule for gammas does the rest with no new integration.

## The density of the sum: $\chi^2_k$ is Gamma$(\tfrac k2,\tfrac12)$ {#sec-chisq-k}

::: {#thm-chisq}
**Theorem (chi-square density).** The $\chi^2_k$ distribution of @def-chisq is exactly
Gamma$\bigl(\tfrac k2,\tfrac12\bigr)$, with density
$$
f_k(x) = \frac{1}{2^{k/2}\,\Gamma(k/2)}\, x^{k/2-1} e^{-x/2},
\qquad x>0.
$$ {#eq-chisq-density}
:::

::: {.proof}
By @lem-chisq1 each $Z_i^2$ is Gamma$(\tfrac12,\tfrac12)$, and the $Z_i$ are independent so
the $Z_i^2$ are independent. The imported addition rule (box (ii) in @sec-borrow) says
independent gammas with a *common rate* add their shapes. All $k$ pieces share the rate
$\beta=\tfrac12$, so
$$
Q = \sum_{i=1}^k Z_i^2 \sim \text{Gamma}\Bigl(\underbrace{\tfrac12+\tfrac12+\dots+\tfrac12}_{k\text{ terms}},\ \tfrac12\Bigr)
= \text{Gamma}\Bigl(\tfrac k2,\ \tfrac12\Bigr).
$$
Substitute $\alpha=\tfrac k2$ and $\beta=\tfrac12$ into the gamma density @eq-gammadens:
$$
f_k(x) = \frac{(1/2)^{k/2}}{\Gamma(k/2)}\, x^{k/2-1} e^{-x/2}
= \frac{1}{2^{k/2}\Gamma(k/2)}\, x^{k/2-1} e^{-x/2},
$$
which is @eq-chisq-density. $\qquad\blacksquare$
:::

::: {.callout-tip title="You might be asked"}
*"Where does the addition rule for gammas come from — isn't that the thing being assumed?"*
It is the convolution of densities, and it is proved in full in [PROB-14] (and again, by the
slicker route, via moment generating functions in [PROB-20]). The complete logical chain is:
the MGF of Gamma$(\alpha,\beta)$ is $(1-t/\beta)^{-\alpha}$, MGFs of independent sums
multiply, and $(1-t/\beta)^{-\alpha_1}(1-t/\beta)^{-\alpha_2}=(1-t/\beta)^{-(\alpha_1+\alpha_2)}$
is the MGF of Gamma$(\alpha_1+\alpha_2,\beta)$ — same rate, added shapes. We flag it as the
one imported ingredient rather than re-derive the gamma MGF here, because it belongs to the
gamma's own article; everything *chi-square-specific* we have derived from scratch.
:::

## Mean and variance, two ways {#sec-chisq-moments}

The chi-square's mean and variance are quoted everywhere as $k$ and $2k$. We derive them
directly from the construction, which is more illuminating than reading them off the gamma.

::: {#thm-chisq-mom}
**Theorem.** If $Q\sim\chi^2_k$ then $\mathbb E[Q]=k$ and $\operatorname{Var}(Q)=2k$.
:::

::: {.proof}
By linearity of expectation (from [PROB-05], which holds with or without independence),
$\mathbb E[Q]=\sum_{i=1}^k \mathbb E[Z_i^2]$. For one standard normal,
$\mathbb E[Z^2]=\operatorname{Var}(Z)+(\mathbb E[Z])^2 = 1 + 0 = 1$, using the
computational identity $\operatorname{Var}(Z)=\mathbb E[Z^2]-(\mathbb E[Z])^2$ from [PROB-06]
and the standard-normal facts $\mathbb E[Z]=0$, $\operatorname{Var}(Z)=1$. Hence
$$
\mathbb E[Q] = \sum_{i=1}^k 1 = k.
$$
For the variance, the $Z_i^2$ are independent (functions of independent variables), so
their variances add (the i.i.d. sum rule from [PROB-08]):
$\operatorname{Var}(Q)=\sum_{i=1}^k \operatorname{Var}(Z_i^2)$. We need
$\operatorname{Var}(Z^2)=\mathbb E[Z^4]-(\mathbb E[Z^2])^2 = \mathbb E[Z^4]-1$. The fourth
moment of a standard normal is $\mathbb E[Z^4]=3$, which we compute now so nothing is left
implicit. Integrate by parts:
$$
\mathbb E[Z^4] = \int_{-\infty}^\infty z^4\,\frac{1}{\sqrt{2\pi}}e^{-z^2/2}\,dz.
$$
Write the integrand as $z^3\cdot\bigl(z\,\tfrac{1}{\sqrt{2\pi}}e^{-z^2/2}\bigr)$. The second
factor is the derivative of $-\tfrac{1}{\sqrt{2\pi}}e^{-z^2/2}$ (since
$\frac{d}{dz}e^{-z^2/2}=-z e^{-z^2/2}$). Integration by parts with $u=z^3$,
$dv = z\,\tfrac{1}{\sqrt{2\pi}}e^{-z^2/2}dz$, so $du=3z^2dz$ and
$v=-\tfrac{1}{\sqrt{2\pi}}e^{-z^2/2}$:
$$
\mathbb E[Z^4] = \Bigl[-z^3\tfrac{1}{\sqrt{2\pi}}e^{-z^2/2}\Bigr]_{-\infty}^{\infty}
+ \int_{-\infty}^\infty 3z^2\,\tfrac{1}{\sqrt{2\pi}}e^{-z^2/2}\,dz.
$$
The boundary term vanishes (the exponential crushes the polynomial $z^3$ at $\pm\infty$),
and the remaining integral is $3\,\mathbb E[Z^2]=3\cdot 1 = 3$. So $\mathbb E[Z^4]=3$, hence
$\operatorname{Var}(Z^2)=3-1=2$, and
$$
\operatorname{Var}(Q) = \sum_{i=1}^k 2 = 2k. \qquad\blacksquare
$$
:::

So the chi-square sits at $k$ and spreads like $\sqrt{2k}$. The mean growing linearly while
the standard deviation grows like $\sqrt k$ is exactly why the distribution looks more
concentrated *relative to its center* as $k$ grows — the seed of its approach to normality
(a consequence of the central limit theorem applied to the sum of i.i.d. $Z_i^2$ terms; the
CLT itself is owned by Statistical Inference A2).

## The payoff: the distribution of the sample variance {#sec-samplevar}

This is the result the whole inference apparatus rests on, and the original reason Helmert
and Pearson cared. Let $X_1,\dots,X_n$ be i.i.d. $\mathcal N(\mu,\sigma^2)$. Define the
**sample mean** and **sample variance**
$$
\bar X = \frac1n\sum_{i=1}^n X_i,
\qquad
s^2 = \frac{1}{n-1}\sum_{i=1}^n (X_i-\bar X)^2.
$$ {#eq-samplevar}

::: {#thm-samplevar}
**Theorem (Helmert).** For i.i.d. normal data,
$$
\frac{(n-1)s^2}{\sigma^2} = \frac{1}{\sigma^2}\sum_{i=1}^n (X_i-\bar X)^2 \sim \chi^2_{n-1},
$$ {#eq-samplevar-chi}
and moreover $\bar X$ and $s^2$ are **independent**.
:::

We do not prove the full theorem from scratch — the clean proof uses an orthogonal change
of variables (a rotation) on the random vector $(X_1,\dots,X_n)$, which is the linear-algebra
machinery of [PROB-17]–[PROB-18] and the Geometry-of-Data track. But we can derive the
**degrees-of-freedom count** completely here, because it is the part that confuses everyone
and it needs no linear algebra — only the algebraic identity that splits the sum of squares.

::: {.proof}
*(The $n-1$ count, fully derived; the rotation step flagged.)* Start by standardizing each
observation: $Z_i = (X_i-\mu)/\sigma$ is standard normal, so
$\sum_{i=1}^n Z_i^2 \sim \chi^2_n$ exactly by @def-chisq. Now decompose this sum around the
sample mean. Write $\bar Z = \frac1n\sum Z_i = (\bar X-\mu)/\sigma$. Add and subtract
$\bar Z$ inside each square:
$$
\sum_{i=1}^n Z_i^2 = \sum_{i=1}^n \bigl[(Z_i-\bar Z)+\bar Z\bigr]^2
= \sum_{i=1}^n (Z_i-\bar Z)^2 + 2\bar Z\sum_{i=1}^n (Z_i-\bar Z) + \sum_{i=1}^n \bar Z^2.
$$
The cross term vanishes because $\sum (Z_i-\bar Z) = \sum Z_i - n\bar Z = n\bar Z - n\bar Z = 0$
(the deviations from the mean always sum to zero — this is the same fact that powers the
normal equations in [REGR-01]). The last term is $n\bar Z^2$. So we have the exact identity
$$
\underbrace{\sum_{i=1}^n Z_i^2}_{\chi^2_n}
= \underbrace{\sum_{i=1}^n (Z_i-\bar Z)^2}_{=\,(n-1)s^2/\sigma^2}
+ \underbrace{n\bar Z^2}_{=\,\bigl((\bar X-\mu)/(\sigma/\sqrt n)\bigr)^2}.
$$ {#eq-cochran}
Identify the last term: $n\bar Z^2 = \bigl(\sqrt n\,\bar Z\bigr)^2 = \bigl(\frac{\bar X-\mu}{\sigma/\sqrt n}\bigr)^2$,
and since $\bar X\sim\mathcal N(\mu,\sigma^2/n)$ (the sample-mean fact from [PROB-13]'s
closure under linear combinations), the quantity $\frac{\bar X-\mu}{\sigma/\sqrt n}$ is
standard normal, so $n\bar Z^2\sim\chi^2_1$. And the middle term, by the definition of $s^2$
in @eq-samplevar, equals $\sum(Z_i-\bar Z)^2 = \frac{1}{\sigma^2}\sum(X_i-\bar X)^2 = (n-1)s^2/\sigma^2$.

So @eq-cochran reads: **a $\chi^2_n$ equals a $\chi^2_1$ plus the quantity we care about.**
If we knew the two right-hand pieces were independent, then by matching moment generating
functions (or @thm-chisq's addition rule run *backwards*), the remaining piece must be
$\chi^2_{n-1}$: it is the only distribution whose independent sum with a $\chi^2_1$ gives a
$\chi^2_n$, since $\tfrac n2 = \tfrac12 + \tfrac{n-1}{2}$ partitions the shape. The
independence of $\bar X$ (hence of $n\bar Z^2$) from $\sum(Z_i-\bar Z)^2$ is the one fact we
import: it follows from rotating the vector $(Z_1,\dots,Z_n)$ by an orthogonal matrix whose
first row is $(1,\dots,1)/\sqrt n$, which sends $\bar Z$-information into one coordinate and
the deviations into the other $n-1$, and a rotation of independent standard normals yields
independent standard normals (the rotational invariance of the multivariate normal,
[PROB-17]). The intuition for the lost degree of freedom is exactly this: **we spent one
degree of freedom estimating the mean.** Of the $n$ original free coordinates, the
constraint $\sum(Z_i-\bar Z)=0$ removes one, leaving $n-1$ free squared directions.
$\qquad\blacksquare$
:::

::: {.callout-important title="Why $n-1$, in one sentence"}
You started with $n$ independent squared deviations, but you measured them around the
*estimated* mean $\bar X$ instead of the true $\mu$; estimating that one number ties the
deviations together with the single linear constraint $\sum(X_i-\bar X)=0$, costing exactly
one degree of freedom and leaving $\chi^2_{n-1}$. This is *the* reason the sample variance
divides by $n-1$ and not $n$: it is what makes $\mathbb E[s^2]=\sigma^2$ (taking the
expectation of @eq-samplevar-chi, $\mathbb E[(n-1)s^2/\sigma^2]=n-1$, so
$\mathbb E[s^2]=\sigma^2$ — unbiased).
:::

Let us see @thm-samplevar empirically before we use it: simulate many normal samples,
compute $(n-1)s^2/\sigma^2$ for each, and check the histogram lands on $\chi^2_{n-1}$.

```{python}
#| label: fig-samplevar
#| fig-cap: "The sample variance is chi-square. We draw $20{,}000$ samples of size $n=6$ from $\\mathcal N(10, 2^2)$, and for each compute $(n-1)s^2/\\sigma^2$. The histogram (blue) lands exactly on the $\\chi^2_{5}$ density (black), confirming @thm-samplevar with $n-1=5$ degrees of freedom. The right panel confirms the *independence* claim: a scatter of $\\bar X$ against $s^2$ across samples shows no relationship — knowing the sample mean tells you nothing about the sample variance, a property unique to the normal."
rng = np.random.default_rng(15)
n, mu, sigma = 6, 10.0, 2.0
N = 20000
samples = rng.normal(mu, sigma, size=(N, n))
xbar = samples.mean(axis=1)
s2 = samples.var(axis=1, ddof=1)
stat = (n - 1) * s2 / sigma**2

fig, (axL, axR) = plt.subplots(1, 2, figsize=(9.2, 3.7))
axL.hist(stat, bins=60, density=True, color=CHI, alpha=0.55, label=r"$(n-1)s^2/\sigma^2$")
xx = np.linspace(0.01, 25, 300)
axL.plot(xx, stats.chi2.pdf(xx, n - 1), color="black", lw=2, label=r"$\chi^2_5$ density")
axL.set_xlabel("value"); axL.set_ylabel("density")
axL.set_title(r"Sample variance follows $\chi^2_{n-1}$"); axL.legend(fontsize=9)

axR.scatter(xbar[:1500], s2[:1500], s=6, color=GREY, alpha=0.5)
axR.set_xlabel(r"sample mean $\bar X$"); axR.set_ylabel(r"sample variance $s^2$")
axR.set_title(r"$\bar X \perp s^2$ (no relationship)")
print(f"empirical mean of (n-1)s^2/sigma^2 = {stat.mean():.3f}  (theory: n-1 = {n-1})")
print(f"empirical var  of (n-1)s^2/sigma^2 = {stat.var():.3f}  (theory: 2(n-1) = {2*(n-1)})")
plt.tight_layout()
```

# Student's $t$ distribution {#sec-t}

## Definition and the reason it exists {#sec-t-def}

::: {#def-t}
**Definition (Student's $t$).** Let $Z\sim\mathcal N(0,1)$ and $V\sim\chi^2_k$ be
**independent**. The **$t$ distribution with $k$ degrees of freedom** is the distribution of
$$
T = \frac{Z}{\sqrt{V/k}}.
$$ {#eq-t-def}
:::

The independence is not decoration; the construction is meaningless without it, and we will
see in @sec-t-applied that it is exactly what @thm-samplevar's independence of $\bar X$ and
$s^2$ supplies. First, *why this ratio*. Recall the problem: we want the distribution of
$$
T = \frac{\bar X - \mu}{s/\sqrt n}.
$$
Divide numerator and denominator by $\sigma/\sqrt n$. The numerator becomes
$\frac{\bar X-\mu}{\sigma/\sqrt n}=Z$, a standard normal. The denominator becomes
$\frac{s/\sqrt n}{\sigma/\sqrt n}=\frac{s}{\sigma}=\sqrt{\frac{s^2}{\sigma^2}}
=\sqrt{\frac{(n-1)s^2/\sigma^2}{n-1}}=\sqrt{V/k}$ with $V=(n-1)s^2/\sigma^2\sim\chi^2_{n-1}$
and $k=n-1$. So the real statistic *is* @eq-t-def. The $t$ is precisely the $z$-score after
you replace the known $\sigma$ by an estimate built from an independent chi-square.

## The density {#sec-t-density}

::: {#thm-t-density}
**Theorem ($t$ density).** $T$ in @def-t has density
$$
f_T(t) = \frac{\Gamma\!\bigl(\tfrac{k+1}{2}\bigr)}{\sqrt{k\pi}\,\Gamma\!\bigl(\tfrac{k}{2}\bigr)}
\left(1+\frac{t^2}{k}\right)^{-\frac{k+1}{2}},
\qquad t\in\mathbb R.
$$ {#eq-t-density}
:::

::: {.proof}
We find the density of $T=Z/\sqrt{V/k}$ by conditioning on $V$ and then averaging over $V$ —
the law of total probability for densities. The plan: for a *fixed* value $V=v$, the
denominator $\sqrt{v/k}$ is a constant, so $T$ is a scaled normal; then we integrate over
the distribution of $v$.

**Step 1 — condition on $V=v$.** Hold $V=v$ fixed. Then $T = Z/\sqrt{v/k}$, which is the
standard normal $Z$ multiplied by the constant $c=1/\sqrt{v/k}=\sqrt{k/v}$. A constant times
a standard normal is normal with variance $c^2=k/v$, so its conditional density is
$$
f_{T\mid V}(t\mid v) = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2\pi (k/v)}}\exp\!\Bigl(-\frac{t^2}{2(k/v)}\Bigr)
= \sqrt{\frac{v}{2\pi k}}\,\exp\!\Bigl(-\frac{v t^2}{2k}\Bigr).
$$
(We used the scaling rule for densities: if $T=cZ$ then
$f_T(t)=\frac1{|c|}\varphi(t/c)$, which for $c=\sqrt{k/v}$ gives the line above.)

**Step 2 — average over $V$.** The unconditional density is the conditional density
weighted by the density of $V$ and integrated:
$$
f_T(t) = \int_0^\infty f_{T\mid V}(t\mid v)\, f_V(v)\,dv,
$$
where $f_V$ is the $\chi^2_k$ density @eq-chisq-density,
$f_V(v)=\frac{1}{2^{k/2}\Gamma(k/2)}v^{k/2-1}e^{-v/2}$. Substitute both:
$$
f_T(t) = \int_0^\infty
\sqrt{\frac{v}{2\pi k}}\,e^{-v t^2/(2k)}
\cdot \frac{1}{2^{k/2}\Gamma(k/2)}\,v^{k/2-1}e^{-v/2}\,dv.
$$
Pull every constant (anything without $v$) outside the integral and collect the powers of
$v$ ($v^{1/2}\cdot v^{k/2-1}=v^{(k+1)/2-1}$) and the exponentials
($e^{-vt^2/(2k)}e^{-v/2}=e^{-v(t^2/k+1)/2}$):
$$
f_T(t) = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2\pi k}\,2^{k/2}\Gamma(k/2)}
\int_0^\infty v^{\frac{k+1}{2}-1}\,
\exp\!\Bigl(-\tfrac{v}{2}\bigl(1+\tfrac{t^2}{k}\bigr)\Bigr)\,dv.
$$
**Step 3 — recognize the integral as a gamma integral.** The integral has the form
$\int_0^\infty v^{a-1}e^{-bv}dv$ with $a=\tfrac{k+1}{2}$ and $b=\tfrac12(1+\tfrac{t^2}{k})$.
That integral equals $\Gamma(a)/b^{a}$ — this is the gamma function @eq-gammafn after the
substitution $u=bv$ (then $v=u/b$, $dv=du/b$, and $\int_0^\infty (u/b)^{a-1}e^{-u}du/b
= b^{-a}\int_0^\infty u^{a-1}e^{-u}du = b^{-a}\Gamma(a)$). So the integral equals
$$
\Gamma\!\Bigl(\tfrac{k+1}{2}\Bigr)\Bigl[\tfrac12\bigl(1+\tfrac{t^2}{k}\bigr)\Bigr]^{-\frac{k+1}{2}}
= \Gamma\!\Bigl(\tfrac{k+1}{2}\Bigr)\,2^{\frac{k+1}{2}}\bigl(1+\tfrac{t^2}{k}\bigr)^{-\frac{k+1}{2}}.
$$
**Step 4 — assemble and cancel.** Multiply by the constant out front:
$$
f_T(t) = \frac{2^{\frac{k+1}{2}}\,\Gamma\!\bigl(\tfrac{k+1}{2}\bigr)}{\sqrt{2\pi k}\,2^{k/2}\Gamma(k/2)}
\bigl(1+\tfrac{t^2}{k}\bigr)^{-\frac{k+1}{2}}.
$$
Collect the powers of $2$: $\dfrac{2^{(k+1)/2}}{2^{k/2}} = 2^{1/2}=\sqrt2$, and
$\dfrac{\sqrt2}{\sqrt{2\pi k}} = \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{\pi k}}$. Hence
$$
f_T(t) = \frac{\Gamma\!\bigl(\tfrac{k+1}{2}\bigr)}{\sqrt{\pi k}\,\Gamma(k/2)}
\bigl(1+\tfrac{t^2}{k}\bigr)^{-\frac{k+1}{2}},
$$
which is @eq-t-density. $\qquad\blacksquare$
:::

Notice the density depends on $t$ only through $t^2$, so it is **symmetric about $0$** — a
visible consequence of $Z$ being symmetric and the denominator being positive. And it is a
power law in $t$ for large $|t|$: as $|t|\to\infty$,
$f_T(t)\sim |t|^{-(k+1)}$, a *polynomial* decay, whereas the normal decays like
$e^{-t^2/2}$. Polynomial tails are vastly heavier than exponential ones — that is the
mathematical content of "heavy tails."

## Why the tails are heavy, and why $t\to$ normal {#sec-t-tails}

::: {#thm-t-limit}
**Theorem ($t_k\to\mathcal N(0,1)$).** As $k\to\infty$, the $t_k$ density converges
pointwise to the standard-normal density $\varphi(t)=\frac{1}{\sqrt{2\pi}}e^{-t^2/2}$.
:::

::: {.proof}
We take the limit of @eq-t-density factor by factor. The key is the standard Calc-1 limit
$$
\lim_{k\to\infty}\Bigl(1+\frac{t^2}{k}\Bigr)^{-\frac{k+1}{2}} = e^{-t^2/2}.
$$
To see it, take logs: $-\tfrac{k+1}{2}\ln(1+\tfrac{t^2}{k})$. For large $k$,
$\ln(1+\tfrac{t^2}{k})\to \tfrac{t^2}{k}-\tfrac12(\tfrac{t^2}{k})^2+\dots$, and the leading
term gives $-\tfrac{k+1}{2}\cdot\tfrac{t^2}{k} = -\tfrac{t^2}{2}\cdot\tfrac{k+1}{k}\to-\tfrac{t^2}{2}$
(higher terms vanish faster), so the log tends to $-t^2/2$ and the expression to
$e^{-t^2/2}$. This is the same $(1+a/k)^k\to e^a$ limit that defines $e$.

For the constant, we need
$\frac{\Gamma((k+1)/2)}{\sqrt{\pi k}\,\Gamma(k/2)} \to \frac{1}{\sqrt{2\pi}}$. The ratio
$\Gamma((k+1)/2)/\Gamma(k/2)$ grows like $\sqrt{k/2}$ for large $k$ (the gamma function's
ratio of consecutive half-arguments behaves like the square root of the argument; this is
the asymptotic $\Gamma(x+\frac12)/\Gamma(x)\sim\sqrt x$, a known property of the gamma
function from [PROB-14]). Then
$\frac{\sqrt{k/2}}{\sqrt{\pi k}} = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2\pi}}$. Multiplying the two limits, the
$t_k$ density tends to $\frac{1}{\sqrt{2\pi}}e^{-t^2/2}=\varphi(t)$. $\qquad\blacksquare$
:::

The probabilistic reading is cleaner than the algebra. The denominator of $T$ is
$\sqrt{V/k}$ where $V/k = \frac1k\sum_{i=1}^k Z_i^2$ is an average of $k$ i.i.d.
unit-mean terms. By the law of large numbers (owned by Inference A2), that average
converges to its mean $1$, so $\sqrt{V/k}\to 1$ and $T\to Z/1 = Z$, a standard normal. For
*small* $k$ the average $V/k$ has real scatter, and on the occasions it comes out small the
ratio $Z/\sqrt{V/k}$ blows up — minting the extreme values that make the tails heavy. The
"degrees of freedom" of the $t$ literally count how many squared normals you averaged to
estimate the scale; more is steadier.

::: {.callout-tip title="You might be asked"}
*"For what $k$ does the $t$ even have a mean? A variance?"* Heavy tails have teeth, and the
honest answer derives both thresholds rather than quoting them.

**The mean exists only for $k>1$, and is then $0$.** A mean $\mathbb E[T]=\int_{-\infty}^\infty t\,f_T(t)\,dt$
*exists* (is finite and well-defined) exactly when the absolute integral
$\int_{-\infty}^\infty |t|\,f_T(t)\,dt$ converges. We showed above that for large $|t|$ the
density behaves like $f_T(t)\sim |t|^{-(k+1)}$, so the integrand behaves like
$|t|\cdot|t|^{-(k+1)} = |t|^{-k}$. By the Calc-1 $p$-test, $\int^\infty |t|^{-k}\,dt$
converges iff the exponent $k>1$. So for $k>1$ the mean exists; for $k\le 1$ it does not.
*When* it exists, its value is $0$: the integrand $t\,f_T(t)$ is an **odd** function of $t$
(because $f_T$ depends on $t$ only through $t^2$, so $f_T(-t)=f_T(t)$, while the factor $t$
flips sign), so the integral over $(-\infty,0)$ exactly cancels the integral over
$(0,\infty)$, giving $0$ — and this cancellation argument is only legitimate *because* the
pieces are separately finite, which is precisely the $k>1$ condition. For $k=1$ — the
**Cauchy distribution** — the integral $\int |t|^{-1}dt$ diverges (logarithmically), so the
mean does not exist at all; you cannot rescue it by symmetry, because $\infty-\infty$ is
undefined.

**The variance exists only for $k>2$, and equals $\frac{k}{k-2}$.** When the mean is $0$,
$\operatorname{Var}(T)=\mathbb E[T^2]$. We compute $\mathbb E[T^2]$ directly from the
construction, reusing the pieces we already have. From @def-t,
$T^2 = Z^2/(V/k) = k\,Z^2\,(1/V)$ with $Z\sim\mathcal N(0,1)$ and $V\sim\chi^2_k$
*independent*, so the expectation factors:
$$
\mathbb E[T^2] = k\,\mathbb E[Z^2]\,\mathbb E\!\left[\tfrac1V\right].
$$
We have $\mathbb E[Z^2]=1$ (from @thm-chisq-mom), and $\mathbb E[1/V]$ for $V\sim\chi^2_k$ is
exactly the reciprocal-chi-square expectation computed in the proof of @thm-f-mean: there we
found $\mathbb E[1/V]=\frac{1}{d_2-2}$ for $V\sim\chi^2_{d_2}$, valid *only when* $d_2>2$
(below that the defining gamma integral diverges). With $d_2=k$ this is
$\mathbb E[1/V]=\frac{1}{k-2}$, finite iff $k>2$. Therefore
$$
\operatorname{Var}(T) = \mathbb E[T^2] = k\cdot 1\cdot\frac{1}{k-2} = \frac{k}{k-2},
\qquad k>2,
$$
which is *larger* than the normal's variance of $1$ (the extra factor $\frac{k}{k-2}>1$ is
the wobble of the estimated ruler) and blows up as $k\downarrow 2$. For $k\le 2$ the
integral $\mathbb E[1/V]$ diverges, so the variance is infinite.

So the $t_1$ (Cauchy) has neither a mean nor a variance — the cleanest example in the
catalog of a distribution whose moments simply fail to exist, exactly the boundary case
flagged in [PROB-05]. An interviewer raising the Cauchy is testing whether you know that
"heavy tails" can mean moments literally do not exist, not merely that they are large.
:::

```{python}
#| label: fig-t-tails
#| fig-cap: "Why the $t$ is the careful choice for small samples. **Left:** $t_3$ (red) versus $\\mathcal N(0,1)$ (purple). Same center and symmetry, but the $t$ is lower in the middle and carries visibly more mass in the tails. **Right:** the right-tail probability $P(T>t)$ on a log scale. The $t_3$ tail (red) sits far above the normal (purple) for large $t$ — at $t=4$ the normal assigns probability $3\\times10^{-5}$ but $t_3$ assigns about $1.3\\times10^{-2}$, roughly 400 times more. Using normal critical values on small-sample $t$-statistics is exactly this overconfidence."
fig, (axL, axR) = plt.subplots(1, 2, figsize=(9.2, 3.7))
xt = np.linspace(-5, 5, 400)
axL.plot(xt, stats.t.pdf(xt, 3), color=TCOL, label=r"$t_3$")
axL.plot(xt, stats.norm.pdf(xt), color=NORM, ls="--", label=r"$\mathcal N(0,1)$")
axL.fill_between(xt, stats.t.pdf(xt, 3), stats.norm.pdf(xt),
                 where=(np.abs(xt) > 2), color=TCOL, alpha=0.18)
axL.set_xlabel("$t$"); axL.set_ylabel("density"); axL.set_title("Heavier tails, lower peak")
axL.legend(fontsize=9)

tt = np.linspace(0, 5, 200)
axR.semilogy(tt, stats.t.sf(tt, 3), color=TCOL, label=r"$P(t_3 > t)$")
axR.semilogy(tt, stats.norm.sf(tt), color=NORM, ls="--", label=r"$P(Z > t)$")
axR.set_xlabel("$t$"); axR.set_ylabel("right-tail probability (log)")
axR.set_title("Tail mass: $t$ vs. normal"); axR.legend(fontsize=9)
print(f"P(Z>4)   = {stats.norm.sf(4):.2e}")
print(f"P(t_3>4) = {stats.t.sf(4,3):.2e}   ratio = {stats.t.sf(4,3)/stats.norm.sf(4):.0f}x")
plt.tight_layout()
```

# The $F$ distribution {#sec-f}

## Definition and meaning {#sec-f-def}

::: {#def-f}
**Definition ($F$).** Let $U\sim\chi^2_{d_1}$ and $V\sim\chi^2_{d_2}$ be **independent**.
The **$F$ distribution with $(d_1,d_2)$ degrees of freedom** is the distribution of
$$
F = \frac{U/d_1}{V/d_2}.
$$ {#eq-f-def}
The order matters: $d_1$ is the **numerator** degrees of freedom, $d_2$ the **denominator**.
:::

Each chi-square is divided by its own degrees of freedom *before* the ratio, so that
$\mathbb E[U/d_1]=1$ and $\mathbb E[V/d_2]=1$ (since $\mathbb E[\chi^2_d]=d$). The $F$ is
therefore a ratio of two quantities each centered at $1$: if the two underlying variances
are equal, $F$ hovers near $1$, and a value far from $1$ is evidence the numerator's spread
genuinely exceeds the denominator's. That is precisely the question "does this group / this
model have more variability than that one?"

## The density {#sec-f-density}

::: {#thm-f-density}
**Theorem ($F$ density).** $F$ in @def-f has density, for $x>0$,
$$
f_F(x) = \frac{\Gamma\!\bigl(\tfrac{d_1+d_2}{2}\bigr)}{\Gamma\!\bigl(\tfrac{d_1}{2}\bigr)\Gamma\!\bigl(\tfrac{d_2}{2}\bigr)}
\left(\frac{d_1}{d_2}\right)^{d_1/2}
\frac{x^{d_1/2-1}}{\bigl(1+\tfrac{d_1}{d_2}x\bigr)^{(d_1+d_2)/2}}.
$$ {#eq-f-density}
:::

::: {.proof}
*(The ratio-of-densities calculation, every step shown.)* Let $R=U/V$ first; we will rescale
to $F$ at the end. Both $U$ and $V$ have chi-square densities @eq-chisq-density. We want the
density of the ratio $R=U/V$ of two independent positive variables; the standard tool is the
**ratio formula** $f_R(r)=\int_0^\infty f_U(rv)\,f_V(v)\,v\,dv$ (the $v$ is the Jacobian of
the change from $(U,V)$ to $(R=U/V,\,V)$; we derive it inline: with $u=rv$ and $v=v$, the map
has Jacobian determinant $\bigl|\partial(u,v)/\partial(r,v)\bigr| = \bigl|\begin{smallmatrix} v & r\\ 0 & 1\end{smallmatrix}\bigr| = v$, and the joint density of $(R,V)$ is the joint density of $(U,V)$ at $(rv,v)$ times $v$; integrating out $v$ gives the formula).

Write $c_1 = \frac{1}{2^{d_1/2}\Gamma(d_1/2)}$ and $c_2=\frac{1}{2^{d_2/2}\Gamma(d_2/2)}$ for
the two chi-square normalizing constants. Then
$$
f_R(r) = \int_0^\infty c_1 (rv)^{d_1/2-1}e^{-rv/2}\cdot c_2 v^{d_2/2-1}e^{-v/2}\cdot v\,dv.
$$
Pull out $c_1 c_2 r^{d_1/2-1}$ and collect powers of $v$
($v^{d_1/2-1}\cdot v^{d_2/2-1}\cdot v = v^{(d_1+d_2)/2-1}$) and the exponentials
($e^{-rv/2}e^{-v/2}=e^{-v(r+1)/2}$):
$$
f_R(r) = c_1 c_2\, r^{d_1/2-1}\int_0^\infty v^{\frac{d_1+d_2}{2}-1}e^{-v(r+1)/2}\,dv.
$$
The integral is again a gamma integral $\int_0^\infty v^{a-1}e^{-bv}dv=\Gamma(a)/b^a$ with
$a=\tfrac{d_1+d_2}{2}$, $b=\tfrac{r+1}{2}$, so it equals
$\Gamma\!\bigl(\tfrac{d_1+d_2}{2}\bigr)\bigl(\tfrac{r+1}{2}\bigr)^{-(d_1+d_2)/2}
=\Gamma\!\bigl(\tfrac{d_1+d_2}{2}\bigr)2^{(d_1+d_2)/2}(r+1)^{-(d_1+d_2)/2}$. Hence
$$
f_R(r) = \frac{2^{(d_1+d_2)/2}}{2^{d_1/2}2^{d_2/2}}\cdot
\frac{\Gamma\!\bigl(\tfrac{d_1+d_2}{2}\bigr)}{\Gamma(d_1/2)\Gamma(d_2/2)}\,
r^{d_1/2-1}(1+r)^{-(d_1+d_2)/2}.
$$
The powers of $2$ cancel completely ($2^{(d_1+d_2)/2}/(2^{d_1/2}2^{d_2/2})=1$), leaving the
density of $R=U/V$:
$$
f_R(r) = \frac{\Gamma\!\bigl(\tfrac{d_1+d_2}{2}\bigr)}{\Gamma(d_1/2)\Gamma(d_2/2)}\,
r^{d_1/2-1}(1+r)^{-(d_1+d_2)/2}.
$$
Finally rescale to $F=\frac{U/d_1}{V/d_2}=\frac{d_2}{d_1}\cdot\frac{U}{V}=\frac{d_2}{d_1}R$,
so $R = \frac{d_1}{d_2}F$. The density transforms by $f_F(x)=f_R\!\bigl(\tfrac{d_1}{d_2}x\bigr)\cdot\tfrac{d_1}{d_2}$
(the scaling rule, Jacobian $d_1/d_2$). Substituting $r=\tfrac{d_1}{d_2}x$:
$$
f_F(x) = \frac{\Gamma\!\bigl(\tfrac{d_1+d_2}{2}\bigr)}{\Gamma(d_1/2)\Gamma(d_2/2)}
\Bigl(\tfrac{d_1}{d_2}x\Bigr)^{d_1/2-1}\Bigl(1+\tfrac{d_1}{d_2}x\Bigr)^{-(d_1+d_2)/2}\cdot\frac{d_1}{d_2}.
$$
Combine the two leading $\tfrac{d_1}{d_2}$ powers: $\bigl(\tfrac{d_1}{d_2}\bigr)^{d_1/2-1}\cdot\tfrac{d_1}{d_2}
= \bigl(\tfrac{d_1}{d_2}\bigr)^{d_1/2}$, which yields @eq-f-density exactly.
$\qquad\blacksquare$
:::

## The one fact that unifies the family: $t^2 = F_{1,k}$ {#sec-tsq-f}

This is the payoff that makes the three distributions one story.

::: {#thm-tsq}
**Theorem.** If $T\sim t_k$ then $T^2\sim F_{1,k}$.
:::

::: {.proof}
By @def-t, $T = Z/\sqrt{V/k}$ with $Z\sim\mathcal N(0,1)$ and $V\sim\chi^2_k$ independent.
Square it:
$$
T^2 = \frac{Z^2}{V/k} = \frac{Z^2/1}{V/k}.
$$
Now read the right-hand side against @def-f. The numerator $Z^2$ is a single squared
standard normal, which by @lem-chisq1 is $\chi^2_1$, and dividing by its degrees of freedom
$1$ leaves it unchanged: $Z^2 = U/d_1$ with $U\sim\chi^2_1$, $d_1=1$. The denominator $V/k$
is a $\chi^2_k$ divided by its degrees of freedom $k$: $V/k$ with $d_2=k$. And $Z^2$ is
independent of $V$ because $Z$ is independent of $V$. So $T^2$ is exactly the ratio in
@eq-f-def with $(d_1,d_2)=(1,k)$:
$$
T^2 = \frac{\chi^2_1/1}{\chi^2_k/k} \sim F_{1,k}. \qquad\blacksquare
$$
:::

This is why a two-sided $t$-test and the corresponding $F$-test give *identical* p-values:
they are the same statistic, one squared. It also closes the family loop: an $F$ with one
numerator degree of freedom is a squared $t$; a $t$ is a normal over a root-chi-square; a
chi-square is a sum of squared normals. **One generator — the normal — three operations.**

## Mean of the $F$, and a useful reciprocal symmetry {#sec-f-moments}

::: {#thm-f-mean}
**Theorem.** If $F\sim F_{d_1,d_2}$ with $d_2>2$, then $\mathbb E[F]=\dfrac{d_2}{d_2-2}$.
:::

::: {.proof}
Write $F=\frac{U/d_1}{V/d_2}=\frac{d_2}{d_1}\cdot U\cdot\frac1V$ with $U\sim\chi^2_{d_1}$,
$V\sim\chi^2_{d_2}$ independent. Independence lets the expectation factor:
$\mathbb E[F]=\frac{d_2}{d_1}\,\mathbb E[U]\,\mathbb E[1/V]$. We have $\mathbb E[U]=d_1$. The
only new piece is $\mathbb E[1/V]$ for $V\sim\chi^2_{d_2}=\text{Gamma}(\tfrac{d_2}{2},\tfrac12)$.
Compute it from the density:
$$
\mathbb E\!\left[\frac1V\right] = \int_0^\infty \frac1v\cdot \frac{1}{2^{d_2/2}\Gamma(d_2/2)}v^{d_2/2-1}e^{-v/2}\,dv
= \frac{1}{2^{d_2/2}\Gamma(d_2/2)}\int_0^\infty v^{(d_2/2-1)-1}e^{-v/2}\,dv.
$$
The integral is a gamma integral $\int_0^\infty v^{a-1}e^{-bv}dv=\Gamma(a)/b^a$ with
$a=\tfrac{d_2}{2}-1$ and $b=\tfrac12$, equal to $\Gamma(\tfrac{d_2}{2}-1)\,2^{d_2/2-1}$
(this requires $a>0$, i.e. $d_2>2$, or the integral diverges and the mean is infinite). So
$$
\mathbb E\!\left[\frac1V\right]
= \frac{\Gamma(\tfrac{d_2}{2}-1)\,2^{d_2/2-1}}{2^{d_2/2}\Gamma(d_2/2)}
= \frac{1}{2}\cdot\frac{\Gamma(\tfrac{d_2}{2}-1)}{\Gamma(\tfrac{d_2}{2})}.
$$
Apply the recurrence $\Gamma(\alpha)=(\alpha-1)\Gamma(\alpha-1)$ from @eq-gammafn with
$\alpha=\tfrac{d_2}{2}$: $\Gamma(\tfrac{d_2}{2})=(\tfrac{d_2}{2}-1)\Gamma(\tfrac{d_2}{2}-1)$,
so $\frac{\Gamma(d_2/2-1)}{\Gamma(d_2/2)}=\frac{1}{d_2/2-1}=\frac{2}{d_2-2}$. Hence
$\mathbb E[1/V]=\frac12\cdot\frac{2}{d_2-2}=\frac{1}{d_2-2}$, and
$$
\mathbb E[F] = \frac{d_2}{d_1}\cdot d_1\cdot\frac{1}{d_2-2} = \frac{d_2}{d_2-2}.
\qquad\blacksquare
$$
:::

So $\mathbb E[F]=\frac{d_2}{d_2-2}$ is slightly *above* $1$ (the skew pushes the mean past
the mode), approaching $1$ as $d_2\to\infty$. And there is a symmetry worth keeping: from
@def-f, swapping numerator and denominator inverts the ratio, so **$1/F_{d_1,d_2}\sim F_{d_2,d_1}$**
— the reciprocal of an $F$ is an $F$ with the degrees of freedom swapped. This is why tables
historically listed only upper tails: the lower tail of $F_{d_1,d_2}$ is the upper tail of
$F_{d_2,d_1}$.

# Worked examples {#sec-examples}

We now run the three distributions on honest problems, including one at the edge of
applicability where the normality assumption that powers all three quietly fails.

## Example 1 — a small-sample $t$ confidence interval (the Guinness problem) {#sec-ex-t}

A brewer measures the alcohol content (%) of $n=8$ batches from a new yeast strain. The
sample is small — exactly Gosset's regime — and the population standard deviation is
unknown, so the $z$-interval is unavailable and the $t$ is mandatory. We compute the
$95\%$ confidence interval for the true mean alcohol content
$\bar x \pm t_{0.975,\,n-1}\cdot s/\sqrt n$, and contrast it with the (wrong) normal interval
to expose the overconfidence the $t$ corrects.

```{python}
#| label: fig-ex-t
#| fig-cap: "Small-sample inference done right. Eight batch measurements (blue points). The $t_7$-based $95\\%$ confidence interval (red band) is meaningfully wider than the naive normal interval (purple dashed) because, with only $7$ degrees of freedom, the $t$ critical value $2.365$ exceeds the normal's $1.960$ by about $21\\%$. Using the normal here would understate the uncertainty — declaring the mean pinned down more tightly than eight noisy measurements can justify."
rng = np.random.default_rng(108)
data = np.array([4.6, 5.1, 4.8, 5.4, 4.9, 5.2, 4.7, 5.0])
n = len(data)
xbar = data.mean(); s = data.std(ddof=1)
se = s / np.sqrt(n)
t_crit = stats.t.ppf(0.975, n - 1)
z_crit = stats.norm.ppf(0.975)
ci_t = (xbar - t_crit * se, xbar + t_crit * se)
ci_z = (xbar - z_crit * se, xbar + z_crit * se)

fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(7.2, 3.4))
ax.scatter(data, np.zeros_like(data), color=CHI, s=55, zorder=3, label="batch measurements")
ax.axvline(xbar, color="black", lw=1.4, label=fr"$\bar x={xbar:.3f}$")
ax.fill_betweenx([-0.4, 0.4], ci_t[0], ci_t[1], color=TCOL, alpha=0.18,
                 label=fr"$t_7$ 95% CI [{ci_t[0]:.3f}, {ci_t[1]:.3f}]")
ax.plot(ci_z, [0.25, 0.25], color=NORM, ls="--", lw=2.5,
        label=fr"normal 95% CI [{ci_z[0]:.3f}, {ci_z[1]:.3f}]")
ax.set_yticks([]); ax.set_xlabel("alcohol content (%)")
ax.set_title("$t$ interval is wider — honestly so")
ax.legend(fontsize=8, loc="upper left")
print(f"t critical (df=7) = {t_crit:.3f}   normal critical = {z_crit:.3f}")
print(f"t  interval half-width = {t_crit*se:.4f}")
print(f"normal half-width      = {z_crit*se:.4f}   (too narrow by {100*(t_crit/z_crit-1):.0f}%)")
plt.tight_layout()
```

The interval is built directly on @def-t: the pivot $\frac{\bar X-\mu}{s/\sqrt n}$ is $t_7$,
so $P\bigl(-t_{0.975,7}\le \frac{\bar X-\mu}{s/\sqrt n}\le t_{0.975,7}\bigr)=0.95$, and
inverting the inequality for $\mu$ gives the interval. The honest takeaway: with $n=8$ the
$t$ correction is not a rounding detail — it widens the interval by a fifth.

## Example 2 — comparing two variances with the $F$-test {#sec-ex-f}

A manufacturer runs two machines filling bottles and wants to know whether one is *more
variable* (less consistent) than the other — a variance comparison, not a mean comparison.
With samples of size $n_1, n_2$ from each, the ratio of sample variances $s_1^2/s_2^2$ is,
under equal true variances, an $F_{n_1-1,\,n_2-1}$: each $s_i^2$ contributes a scaled
$\chi^2_{n_i-1}$ by @thm-samplevar, and their ratio (with the $\sigma^2$'s cancelling) is
@def-f.

```{python}
#| label: fig-ex-f
#| fig-cap: "An $F$-test for equality of variances. Machine A ($n_1=12$) and Machine B ($n_2=10$) fill volumes. The observed variance ratio $s_A^2/s_B^2$ (red line) is compared to the $F_{11,9}$ null density (green). The shaded region is the two-sided rejection zone at $5\\%$. Here the ratio falls in the tail: Machine A is significantly more variable, so the $F$-test flags a real difference in consistency that a means-only comparison would miss entirely."
rng = np.random.default_rng(42)
machineA = rng.normal(500, 4.2, size=12)   # more variable
machineB = rng.normal(500, 2.0, size=10)   # tighter
n1, n2 = len(machineA), len(machineB)
sA2 = machineA.var(ddof=1); sB2 = machineB.var(ddof=1)
Fstat = sA2 / sB2
d1, d2 = n1 - 1, n2 - 1
# two-sided p-value
p_upper = stats.f.sf(Fstat, d1, d2)
pval = 2 * min(p_upper, 1 - p_upper)
lo = stats.f.ppf(0.025, d1, d2); hi = stats.f.ppf(0.975, d1, d2)

fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(7.2, 3.8))
xf = np.linspace(0.001, 8, 400)
ax.plot(xf, stats.f.pdf(xf, d1, d2), color=FCOL, label=fr"$F_{{{d1},{d2}}}$ null")
ax.fill_between(xf, stats.f.pdf(xf, d1, d2), where=(xf > hi), color=FCOL, alpha=0.2)
ax.fill_between(xf, stats.f.pdf(xf, d1, d2), where=(xf < lo), color=FCOL, alpha=0.2)
ax.axvline(Fstat, color=TCOL, lw=2.2, label=fr"observed $s_A^2/s_B^2={Fstat:.2f}$")
ax.set_xlabel("$F$ value"); ax.set_ylabel("density")
ax.set_title("Is one machine more variable?"); ax.legend(fontsize=9)
print(f"s_A^2 = {sA2:.2f}   s_B^2 = {sB2:.2f}   F = {Fstat:.3f}")
print(f"95% acceptance region for F_{{{d1},{d2}}}: [{lo:.3f}, {hi:.3f}]")
print(f"two-sided p-value = {pval:.4f}  ->  {'reject' if pval<0.05 else 'fail to reject'} equal variances")
plt.tight_layout()
```

## Example 3 — chi-square goodness of fit, Pearson's original use {#sec-ex-chi}

Pearson invented the chi-square statistic in 1900 to test whether observed counts match a
hypothesized distribution. Roll a die $n=120$ times; under "fair die," each face is expected
$20$ times. The statistic $\sum_j (O_j-E_j)^2/E_j$ — observed-minus-expected, squared,
scaled by expected — is approximately $\chi^2_{6-1}=\chi^2_5$ under the null (five degrees of
freedom: six categories, minus one for the constraint that the counts sum to $120$, the same
"one constraint, one lost degree of freedom" bookkeeping as the sample variance).

```{python}
#| label: fig-ex-chi
#| fig-cap: "Pearson's goodness-of-fit test. A suspected die is rolled $120$ times; observed face counts (bars) deviate from the expected $20$ each (dashed line). The chi-square statistic aggregates these squared, scaled deviations into one number, compared against the $\\chi^2_5$ null (right). The observed statistic (red) lands deep in the tail, so the fair-die hypothesis is rejected. The $\\chi^2_5$ is the *approximate* null because each $(O_j-E_j)/\\sqrt{E_j}$ is approximately standard normal by the CLT for counts — an approximation that needs large expected counts to hold."
rng = np.random.default_rng(2718)
# a loaded die: face 6 favored
true_p = np.array([1, 1, 1, 1, 1, 2.0]); true_p /= true_p.sum()
rolls = rng.choice(np.arange(1, 7), size=120, p=true_p)
O = np.array([(rolls == f).sum() for f in range(1, 7)], dtype=float)
E = np.full(6, 120 / 6)
chi2_stat = np.sum((O - E) ** 2 / E)
df = 6 - 1
pval = stats.chi2.sf(chi2_stat, df)

fig, (axL, axR) = plt.subplots(1, 2, figsize=(9.2, 3.7))
axL.bar(np.arange(1, 7), O, color=CHI, alpha=0.7, label="observed")
axL.axhline(20, color=GREY, ls="--", lw=1.6, label="expected (fair)")
axL.set_xlabel("die face"); axL.set_ylabel("count"); axL.set_title("Observed vs. expected")
axL.legend(fontsize=9)

xx = np.linspace(0.01, 25, 300)
axR.plot(xx, stats.chi2.pdf(xx, df), color=CHI, label=r"$\chi^2_5$ null")
axR.fill_between(xx, stats.chi2.pdf(xx, df), where=(xx > chi2_stat), color=CHI, alpha=0.25)
axR.axvline(chi2_stat, color=TCOL, lw=2.2, label=fr"observed $={chi2_stat:.2f}$")
axR.set_xlabel(r"$\chi^2$ value"); axR.set_ylabel("density"); axR.set_title("Test against the null")
axR.legend(fontsize=9)
print(f"observed counts = {O.astype(int)}")
print(f"chi-square statistic = {chi2_stat:.3f} on df={df}")
print(f"p-value = {pval:.4f}  ->  {'reject' if pval<0.05 else 'fail to reject'} fair die")
plt.tight_layout()
```

## Example 4 — the edge of applicability: when normality fails {#sec-ex-edge}

Every distribution in this article was *manufactured from normal data*. That is the load-
bearing assumption, and the honest boundary is what happens when it is false. Take a small
sample from a **heavy-tailed** population — incomes, insurance claims, file sizes — and form
the $t$-statistic anyway. Because the data are not normal, the sample mean is not normal and
$(n-1)s^2/\sigma^2$ is not chi-square, so the studentized statistic is **not** $t$-distributed.
The danger is specific: heavy tails make the actual statistic *even heavier-tailed than the
nominal $t$*, so a $t$-based interval has less coverage than advertised — you think you have
$95\%$ confidence and you have far less.

```{python}
#| label: fig-ex-edge
#| fig-cap: "Where the machinery breaks. We draw $n=8$ samples from a heavy-tailed log-normal population (not normal) and compute the studentized statistic $(\\bar X-\\mu)/(s/\\sqrt n)$ across $40{,}000$ replications. Its histogram (orange) is compared to the $t_7$ density (red) that the theory assumes. The empirical statistic is **more peaked and far heavier-tailed** than $t_7$: the skew of the population leaks through. A nominal $95\\%$ $t$-interval built on this data actually covers the truth only about the printed fraction of the time — a real, common failure when the normality assumption is taken on faith."
rng = np.random.default_rng(99)
n = 8; M = 40000
# log-normal population: heavy right tail, skewed
sigma_ln = 1.0
mu_pop = np.exp(sigma_ln**2 / 2)  # true mean of lognormal with these params (meanlog=0)
data = rng.lognormal(mean=0.0, sigma=sigma_ln, size=(M, n))
xbar = data.mean(axis=1)
s = data.std(axis=1, ddof=1)
tstat = (xbar - mu_pop) / (s / np.sqrt(n))

# empirical coverage of a nominal 95% t-interval
tc = stats.t.ppf(0.975, n - 1)
covered = np.mean(np.abs(tstat) <= tc)

fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(7.2, 3.8))
grid = np.linspace(-6, 6, 400)
ax.hist(tstat, bins=400, range=(-6, 6), density=True, color=ACCENT, alpha=0.5,
        label="studentized stat (lognormal data)")
ax.plot(grid, stats.t.pdf(grid, n - 1), color=TCOL, lw=2, label=r"assumed $t_7$")
ax.set_xlim(-6, 6); ax.set_xlabel("statistic value"); ax.set_ylabel("density")
ax.set_title("Studentized non-normal data is NOT $t$")
ax.legend(fontsize=9)
print(f"nominal coverage of 95% t-interval = 0.95")
print(f"actual  coverage on lognormal n=8  = {covered:.3f}  (a real shortfall)")
plt.tight_layout()
```

::: {.callout-warning title="Pitfall"}
**The $t$, $\chi^2$, and $F$ are exact only for normal data.** Their entire derivation
started from $Z_i\sim\mathcal N(0,1)$. On non-normal data — especially skewed or
heavy-tailed populations — at small $n$ these distributions can be badly wrong, and the
error is in the *dangerous* direction: intervals too narrow, tests too eager to reject. Two
saving graces and one warning: (1) for the *mean*, the central limit theorem (Inference A2)
rescues you as $n$ grows, so large-sample $t$-procedures are robust; (2) for *variances*,
there is no such rescue — the $\chi^2$ and $F$ tests for variances are **notoriously
non-robust** to non-normality even at large $n$, because they depend on the fourth moment,
not just the second. When normality is doubtful and $n$ is small, reach for the bootstrap
([PROB-22] builds the resampling object; Inference E1 owns the method) or a permutation/
rank-based test instead.
:::

# Failure modes and applicability {#sec-failure}

The examples already exposed the central boundary; here we consolidate the practitioner's
judgment.

- **These are distributions of statistics, not data.** The single most common conceptual
  error is treating "chi-square" or "$t$" as a model you fit to raw observations. You do not.
  They describe the sampling behavior of computed quantities — a variance, a studentized
  mean, a variance ratio — under a normal-data null. (The $t$ *is* occasionally used as a
  heavy-tailed data model — "robust" regression with $t$-distributed errors — but that is a
  deliberate, separate modeling choice, not what this article's $t$ is for.)
- **Degrees of freedom are not a tuning knob.** Each df count is *derived* from a
  constraint: $n-1$ for a sample variance because the mean was estimated; $6-1$ for a
  goodness-of-fit because the total is fixed; $(d_1,d_2)$ for an $F$ from the two chi-squares'
  own counts. Getting df wrong is the most frequent practical mistake, and it is always
  traceable to miscounting constraints.
- **The variance tests are fragile.** As the pitfall warned: $\chi^2$ and $F$ tests *for
  variances* lean on normality far more heavily than $t$-tests for means, and the CLT does
  not save them. Modern practice often prefers Levene's or Brown–Forsythe tests (which use
  absolute deviations and are robust) over the raw $F$-test of variances.
- **When they shine.** Normal or near-normal data, the canonical inference targets — a mean
  with unknown variance ($t$), a single variance or a goodness-of-fit count ($\chi^2$),
  comparing variances or nested regression models ($F$). In that habitat they are *exact*,
  closed-form, and optimal, which is exactly why they have run classical statistics for a
  century.

::: {.callout-tip title="You might be asked"}
*"You have a regression with $p$ predictors and want to test whether a subset of $q$ of them
matters. What distribution, and why?"* The $F$. You fit the full model and a reduced model
(dropping the $q$ predictors), and compare the *extra* sum of squares the full model
explains against the residual variance. The extra sum of squares, scaled, is a $\chi^2_q$
under the null that those predictors are useless; the residual variance is an independent
$\chi^2_{n-p-1}$; their ratio of per-degree-of-freedom pieces is exactly the $F$ of @def-f
with $(d_1,d_2)=(q,\,n-p-1)$. The independence of numerator and denominator — required by
@def-f — comes from the same orthogonality that gave us @thm-samplevar's
$\bar X\perp s^2$. This nested-model $F$-test is owned by [REGR-09] and Inference D2; recognizing
that it is *this* $F$, built from *these* chi-squares, is the connection an interviewer wants.
:::

# Connections and where to go next {#sec-connections}

::: {.callout-important title="Where you are in the track"}
You arrived from the continuous catalog: **[PROB-13]** gave the normal and its standard form
$Z$, and **[PROB-14]** gave the gamma function and density we used to manufacture the
chi-square. This article squared, summed, and ratioed normals into the three sampling
distributions and proved they are one family ($t^2=F_{1,k}$; all are chi-squares; all are
sums of squared normals). The catalog now closes:

- **[PROB-16 *The Exponential Family*]** — the unifying algebraic form behind Bernoulli,
  Poisson, normal, gamma, and beta. An honest boundary it draws: the **Student's $t$ is
  *not* an exponential-family member** (its tails are polynomial, not exponential), which is
  exactly why $t$-based models behave so differently — a forward connection worth carrying.

And the downstream tracks consume these objects directly:

- **[REGR-09]** — the $F$-test for nested regression models, the $t$-tests on individual
  coefficients, and the $\chi^2$ behind the residual sum of squares — all are the
  distributions built here, applied to the regression design.
- **Statistical Inference C2, D2** — the $t$ and $\chi^2$ confidence intervals and the full
  $z/t/\chi^2/F$ testing menu. Inference also owns the **central limit theorem** (A2) that
  governs when these exact-for-normal results survive on non-normal data — the rescue, and
  its limits, flagged throughout this article.
:::

# Going deeper {#sec-deeper}

- **Primary sources.** "Student" (W. S. Gosset), "The Probable Error of a Mean,"
  *Biometrika* 6 (1908) — the $t$, derived and simulated at the Guinness brewery. Karl
  Pearson, "On the Criterion..." *Philosophical Magazine* (1900) — the chi-square
  goodness-of-fit test. R. A. Fisher's work on the analysis of variance in the 1920s, and
  G. W. Snedecor's naming of the $F$ in his honor, *Statistical Methods* (1934). Helmert's
  1870s derivation of the sample-variance distribution predates Pearson's naming.
- **The rabbit hole.** *Cochran's theorem* generalizes the sum-of-squares decomposition in
  @eq-cochran: any quadratic form in normal variables that splits a $\chi^2_n$ into pieces of
  fixed ranks yields independent chi-squares with those ranks as degrees of freedom — the
  engine behind every ANOVA table. It rests squarely on the linear-algebra (rank,
  orthogonal projection) of [PROB-17]–[PROB-18] and the Geometry-of-Data track.
- **Open threads deliberately deferred.** *Why exactly are $\bar X$ and $s^2$ independent for
  normal data — and only for normal data?* The full proof needs the rotational invariance of
  the multivariate normal ([PROB-17]). *The noncentral $\chi^2$, $t$, and $F$* (what these
  become under the alternative hypothesis, governing the *power* of the tests) live with the
  Inference track. *Is the $t$ ever the right model for raw data?* — yes, as a heavy-tailed
  error model, which connects to robust regression in the Regression track.
